mnemonics.title: sigsetjmp Parameters
mnemonics.content: Remember sigsetjmp takes (env, saveMASK) - the SAVEMASK flag decides if signal mask is saved. Think: 'S' for Setjmp, 'S' for Save signal, 'S' for Second parameter = Save.

mnemonics.title: Return Value Pattern
mnemonics.content: 0 means Zero (initial call), Non-zero means Non-zero (jumped back). The value returned is the value passed to longjmp, with 0 becoming 1.

mnemonics.title: sigjmp_buf Contents
mnemonics.content: Think of sigjmp_buf as a 'snapshot' - it captures REGisters, STACK pointer, PROGRAM counter, and SIGNAL MASK.

acronyms.usage: The buffer that stores the execution context including signal mask for non-local jumps
Integer parameter in sigsetjmp that determines whether the signal mask should be saved (0=no, non-zero=yes)
Parameter name for the sigjmp_buf that stores the jump context

visualTips.visualization: Draw two boxes: one for 'Initial call (returns 0)' and one for 'After siglongjmp (returns val)'. The savemask is like a toggle switch - ON means save mask, OFF means don't save.
Imagine a stack of signal flags. sigsetjmp takes a photo of this stack. siglongjmp restores that exact photo - if savemask was set.
Think of sigjmp_buf as a camera snapshot of the program's state at that moment - registers, stack pointer, program counter, and signal mask are all 'photographed'.

analogies.analogy: sigsetjmp is like placing a bookmark in a book. You can always jump back to that exact page later using siglongjmp, restoring exactly where you were.
savemask is like the save game feature in video games. When savemask=1, you save everything including your inventory (signal mask). When savemask=0, you only save your position.
siglongjmp is like teleporting back to a checkpoint. You don't walk back through functions - you instantly appear at the sigsetjmp location with restored state.
